title: Binary search in a sorted array
description: Implement binary search to find the index of a target element in a sorted array

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Initialize i = 0, arr[0] = 2, compare 2 == 6? No → i = 1
Step 2: arr[1] = 4, compare 4 == 6? No → i = 2
Step 3: arr[2] = 6, compare 6 == 6? Yes → Return index 2

Step 1: i = 0, arr[0] = 1, 1 == 4? No → i = 1
Step 2: i = 1, arr[1] = 3, 3 == 4? No → i = 2
Step 3: i = 2, arr[2] = 5, 5 == 4? No → i = 3
Step 4: i = 3, arr[3] = 7, 7 == 4? No → i = 4
Step 5: i = 4, arr[4] = 9, 9 == 4? No → i = 5 (exceeds length)
Step 6: Loop ends, return -1

approaches.name: Approach 1: Linear Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array from start to end, comparing each element to the target until found or the end is reached. Use this only if the array is unsorted or as a baseline for understanding binary search.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Linear Search in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of linear search to find target index in sorted array
ladders.explanation: The linear search approach works by sequentially examining each element in the array from the first to the last position. Even though the array is sorted, we ignore this property and treat it as unsorted for this approach. The intuition is simple: start at the beginning, check each element one by one, and stop when we either find the target or reach the end. This works because we're guaranteed to encounter every element if we traverse the entire array. The algorithm begins by initializing an index variable to 0. We then enter a loop that continues while the index is less than the array length. Inside the loop, we compare the current element with the target. If they match, we immediately return the current index. If not, we increment the index and continue. If the loop completes without finding the target, we return -1 to indicate the target is not present. Time complexity is O(n) because in the worst case (target not present or at the end), we examine all n elements. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a constant amount of extra memory for the index variable. This connects to the parent problem of binary search by serving as a baseline comparison - linear search is simpler to implement but less efficient on sorted arrays. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty arrays (should return -1), off-by-one errors in loop conditions, and not returning -1 when the target isn't found. The real code pattern involves a simple for or while loop with an early return on match.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize i = 0, arr[0] = 2, compare 2 == 6? No → i = 1
Step 2: arr[1] = 4, compare 4 == 6? No → i = 2
Step 3: arr[2] = 6, compare 6 == 6? Yes → Return index 2

Step 1: i = 0, arr[0] = 1, 1 == 4? No → i = 1
Step 2: i = 1, arr[1] = 3, 3 == 4? No → i = 2
Step 3: i = 2, arr[2] = 5, 5 == 4? No → i = 3
Step 4: i = 3, arr[3] = 7, 7 == 4? No → i = 4
Step 5: i = 4, arr[4] = 9, 9 == 4? No → i = 5 (exceeds length)
Step 6: Loop ends, return -1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Array Traversal with Early Exit
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that traverses array and returns immediately on match
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine represents the heart of linear search: the loop that examines each array element and exits as soon as the target is found. The intuition is that we want to minimize unnecessary comparisons by stopping immediately upon success. This works because once we find the target, there's no need to check remaining elements. The algorithm uses a while loop with a condition that checks both that we haven't reached the end AND that we haven't found the target yet. Alternatively, we can use a for loop with an early return statement. The key insight is the early exit optimization - without it, we would always examine all elements even after finding the target. Time complexity remains O(n) in worst case but becomes O(k) where k is the position of the target in best case. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by providing the main search mechanism. Common mistakes include using the wrong loop condition (should be i < n, not i <= n for 0-indexed arrays), forgetting to increment the index in while loops, and placing the return statement incorrectly. The real code pattern involves a loop with a conditional check and return statement inside.

examples.trace:
Step 1: i = 0, arr[0] = 3, 3 == 9? No → i = 1
Step 2: i = 1, arr[1] = 7, 7 == 9? No → i = 2
Step 3: i = 2, arr[2] = 9, 9 == 9? Yes → Exit loop immediately, return 2

Step 1: i = 0, arr[0] = 5, 5 == 20? No → i = 1
Step 2: i = 1, arr[1] = 10, 10 == 20? No → i = 2
Step 3: i = 2, arr[2] = 15, 15 == 20? No → i = 3
Step 4: i = 3 exceeds array length, exit loop, return -1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential Element Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare each array element with target value in order
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operation of comparing the current array element with the target value. The intuition is that searching requires checking for equality between what we have (array elements) and what we want (target). This works because we can determine if we've found the target by direct comparison. The algorithm is simple: at each position i, access arr[i] and compare it to target using the equality operator (==). If equal, we've found our target at position i. If not, we move to the next position. The key insight is that we must perform this comparison at every position until we either find a match or exhaust all positions. Time complexity for this specific operation is O(1) per comparison, but when combined with traversal becomes O(n). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the decision mechanism for when to exit the loop. Common mistakes include using assignment (=) instead of comparison (==), comparing the index i with the target instead of arr[i], and not handling different data types properly. The real code pattern involves arr[i] == target as a boolean expression.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Access arr[1] = 8
Step 2: Compare 8 == 8
Step 3: Result is true

Step 1: Access arr[2] = 12
Step 2: Compare 12 == 8
Step 3: Result is false

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Access array elements using indices in sequential order
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to access individual elements in an array using their indices. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can retrieve any element if we know its position (index). This works because arrays provide constant-time access to any element via its index. The algorithm involves using the array name followed by square brackets containing the index: arr[i]. For linear search, we need to access elements sequentially: arr[0], then arr[1], then arr[2], etc. The key insight is that array indices typically start at 0 (zero-indexed) and go up to length-1. We must ensure we never try to access an index outside this valid range. Time complexity for a single access is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the access operation itself. This feeds into the core logic by providing the mechanism to retrieve elements for comparison. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (assuming first element is at index 1), accessing arr[length] (which is out of bounds), and confusing the index with the element value. The real code pattern involves a loop where i increments from 0 to n-1, with arr[i] accessed each iteration.

examples.trace:
Step 1: arr[0] = 10
Step 2: arr[1] = 20
Step 3: arr[2] = 30
Step 4: arr[3] = 40

Step 1: arr[0] = 5
Step 2: arr[1] = 15
Step 3: arr[2] = 25

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Loop Counter Incrementation
ladders.desc: Increment an index variable to traverse through array positions
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves increasing a counter variable to move through array indices sequentially. The intuition is that to examine each array element, we need to visit each position in order, which requires systematically updating our position. This works because arrays are ordered structures, and we can use a simple integer variable to track our current position. The algorithm starts with i = 0 (first position). After checking arr[i], we increment i by 1 (i = i + 1 or i += 1) to move to the next position. We continue until i reaches the array length. The key insight is that the increment operation must happen after processing the current element but before checking the loop condition again. Time complexity for the increment operation itself is O(1), but when combined with the loop becomes part of O(n) traversal. Space complexity is O(1) for the counter variable. This feeds into array indexing by providing the mechanism to generate successive indices. Common mistakes include incrementing before checking the current element, forgetting to increment (causing infinite loop), or incrementing by wrong amount. The real code pattern involves i += 1 inside a loop.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initial i = 0
Step 2: Increment: i = 0 + 1 = 1
Step 3: Increment: i = 1 + 1 = 2
Step 4: Increment: i = 2 + 1 = 3

Step 1: Initial i = 2
Step 2: Increment: i = 2 + 1 = 3
Step 3: Increment: i = 3 + 1 = 4

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Variable Assignment and Comparison
ladders.desc: Assign values to variables and compare them for equality
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers two basic programming operations: assigning a value to a variable and comparing two values for equality. The intuition is that we need variables to store data (like the target value and current array element) and comparison to make decisions (like whether we found the target). Assignment works by storing a value in a named memory location: target = 5 stores 5 in variable 'target'. Comparison works by evaluating whether two values are equal: target == arr[i] returns true if they match, false otherwise. These operations work because programming languages provide these as fundamental building blocks. The algorithm for assignment is simply variable = value. For comparison, it's value1 == value2. The key insight is that assignment changes what a variable holds, while comparison checks a condition without changing anything. Time and space complexity for both are O(1). This feeds into loop counter incrementation and element comparison by providing the basic operations needed. Common mistakes include confusing = (assignment) with == (comparison), comparing variables of incompatible types, and not initializing variables before use. The real code pattern involves simple statements like i = 0 and if target == current_element.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Execute target = 10 → target now contains 10
Step 2: Evaluate 10 == 10 → true

Step 1: Execute x = 7 → x now contains 7
Step 2: Evaluate 7 == 5 → false

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Maintain left and right pointers to define the search interval, repeatedly calculate the middle index, and adjust pointers based on comparisons with the target. This is the standard efficient method for sorted arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Iterative Binary Search Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of binary search to find target index in sorted array
ladders.explanation: The iterative binary search algorithm efficiently locates a target element in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search interval. The intuition is that because the array is sorted, we can eliminate half of the remaining elements with each comparison. We maintain two pointers, left and right, that define the current search interval. At each step, we calculate the middle index using integer division to avoid overflow (left + (right-left)//2). We compare the middle element to the target: if equal, we return the index; if the middle element is less than the target, we know the target must be in the right half (if present), so we move left to mid+1; if greater, we move right to mid-1. This process continues until either the target is found or the interval becomes empty (left > right), indicating the target is not present. The algorithm runs in O(log n) time because each iteration reduces the search space by half, and uses O(1) space since we only store a few pointers. This connects directly to the key subroutine of maintaining and updating search boundaries. Common mistakes include: 1) Using (left+right)//2 which can cause integer overflow for large arrays, 2) Forgetting to update boundaries correctly (mid±1), 3) Using while left < right instead of left <= right, which misses single-element cases. Real code pattern: def binary_search(arr, target): left, right = 0, len(arr)-1; while left <= right: mid = left + (right-left)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return -1

examples.trace:
Initial: left=0, right=5, arr=[1,3,5,7,9,11], target=7
Step 1: mid = 0+(5-0)//2 = 2, arr[2]=5 < 7 → left=mid+1=3
Step 2: left=3, right=5, mid=3+(5-3)//2=4, arr[4]=9 > 7 → right=mid-1=3
Step 3: left=3, right=3, mid=3+(3-3)//2=3, arr[3]=7 == 7 → return 3

Initial: left=0, right=4, arr=[2,4,6,8,10], target=5
Step 1: mid=0+(4-0)//2=2, arr[2]=6 > 5 → right=mid-1=1
Step 2: left=0, right=1, mid=0+(1-0)//2=0, arr[0]=2 < 5 → left=mid+1=1
Step 3: left=1, right=1, mid=1+(1-1)//2=1, arr[1]=4 < 5 → left=mid+1=2
Step 4: left=2 > right=1 → loop ends, return -1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintaining and Updating Search Boundaries
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that adjusts left/right pointers based on comparisons
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the heart of binary search: managing the search interval [left, right] and updating it based on comparisons with the middle element. The intuition is that we maintain an interval that always contains the target if it exists in the array. At each iteration, we examine the middle element of the current interval. If it equals the target, we're done. If it's less than the target, we know the target (if present) must be to the right because the array is sorted in ascending order, so we discard the left half including the middle element by setting left = mid+1. If it's greater, we discard the right half including the middle by setting right = mid-1. This process guarantees we either find the target or eventually make the interval empty (left > right). The time complexity is O(log n) for the loop, and space is O(1). This feeds into the full problem as the main algorithmic structure. Common mistakes: 1) Using left = mid or right = mid (not ±1) which can cause infinite loops, 2) Incorrect termination condition, 3) Not handling empty intervals properly. Real code pattern: while left <= right: mid = left + (right-left)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1

examples.trace:
Step 1: mid=0+(4-0)//2=2, arr[2]=30 == 30 → return 2
(No further steps needed as target found immediately)

Step 1: mid=0+(3-0)//2=1, arr[1]=2 < 3 → left=mid+1=2
Step 2: left=2, right=3, mid=2+(3-2)//2=2, arr[2]=3 == 3 → return 2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Halving Search Space with Middle Comparison
ladders.desc: Calculate middle index and compare element to decide which half to search
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique of binary search: at each step, we calculate the middle index of the current search interval and compare that element to the target to determine which half of the interval to explore next. The intuition leverages the sorted property: if arr[mid] < target, then every element at indices ≤ mid must also be < target (since array is sorted ascending), so we can safely discard the left half. Conversely, if arr[mid] > target, we discard the right half. If equal, we've found the target. The middle index calculation uses mid = left + (right-left)//2 instead of (left+right)//2 to prevent integer overflow when left and right are large. This operation reduces the problem size by approximately half each time, leading to logarithmic time complexity. This feeds into the boundary update sub-routine as the decision-making step. Common mistakes: 1) Using floating-point division or incorrect integer division, 2) Not understanding why we can discard halves, 3) Off-by-one errors in middle calculation. Real code pattern: mid = left + (right-left)//2; if arr[mid] == target: found; elif arr[mid] < target: search right; else: search left

examples.trace:
Calculate mid: left=0, right=4 → mid=0+(4-0)//2=2
Compare arr[2]=6 to target=6: equal → element found at index 2

Calculate mid: left=0, right=4 → mid=0+(4-0)//2=2
Compare arr[2]=5 to target=7: 5 < 7 → target must be in right half (indices 3-4)

testCases.expected: found at mid=2? Wait recalc: mid=2, arr[2]=3>2 → search left half

ladders.title: Building Block — Calculating Middle Index Safely
ladders.desc: Compute the middle index of an interval without integer overflow
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves calculating the midpoint between two indices left and right in a way that avoids integer overflow. The intuitive approach (left+right)//2 can overflow when left and right are large (close to the maximum integer value), causing incorrect results or errors. The safe formula mid = left + (right-left)//2 achieves the same mathematical result without risk of overflow because (right-left) is smaller than (left+right). Here's why it works mathematically: left + (right-left)//2 = (2*left + right-left)//2 = (left+right)//2. We use integer division (//) to get an integer index. This calculation is fundamental to binary search because we need to examine the middle element of the current search interval. The time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into the core logic as the first step in each iteration. Common mistakes: 1) Using / instead of // resulting in float, 2) Forgetting parentheses in (right-left)//2, 3) Not understanding why the safe formula works. Real code pattern: mid = left + (right-left)//2

examples.trace:
mid = 0 + (10-0)//2 = 0 + 10//2 = 0 + 5 = 5
Alternative: (0+10)//2 = 10//2 = 5 (same result, but safe formula avoids potential overflow)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Elements in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Compare a given element to a target and determine relative position
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves comparing an array element to a target value and determining whether the element is less than, equal to, or greater than the target. In the context of binary search on a sorted ascending array, this comparison tells us where to search next: if element < target, the target (if present) must be to the right; if element > target, the target must be to the left; if equal, we've found it. The intuition is straightforward but crucial: the sorted order guarantees that all elements to the left are ≤ current element and all to the right are ≥ current element. This operation has O(1) time and space complexity. It feeds into the middle comparison as the actual decision mechanism. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing the direction (thinking 'greater than' means search right), 2) Not considering equality case separately, 3) Using incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: if arr[i] == target: return 'equal'; elif arr[i] < target: return 'less'; else: return 'greater'

examples.trace: Element at index 2 is arr[2]=5. Compare 5 to target 5: 5 == 5 → equal
Element at index 1 is arr[1]=4. Compare 4 to target 7: 4 < 7 → less

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding that arrays are ordered collections where each element has a numerical index starting from 0. To access an element, we use the syntax arr[index], which retrieves the value stored at that position. For example, in array [10, 20, 30], arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. The intuition is that arrays provide constant-time O(1) access to any element if you know its index, making them efficient for algorithms like binary search. Indices must be valid integers within the range 0 to len(arr)-1; accessing outside this range causes an error. This foundational operation feeds into all higher levels because binary search constantly accesses array elements at calculated indices. For beginners: think of an array like a row of numbered boxes where each box holds a value. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing (starting at 1), 2) Accessing indices beyond array bounds, 3) Confusing index with value. Real code pattern: element = arr[i] where i is integer index

examples.trace: Array has 4 elements at indices 0,1,2,3. Index 2 refers to the third element (since counting starts at 0). arr[2] = 15
Index 0 is the first element. arr[0] = 100

approaches.name: Approach 3: Recursive Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Define a recursive function that halves the search interval by comparing the middle element to the target and calling itself on the appropriate subarray. This approach is elegant but uses extra space for the call stack.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Binary Search Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete recursive implementation to find target index in sorted array
ladders.explanation: This is the complete recursive binary search solution. The intuition is that by repeatedly halving the search interval, we can find any element in O(log n) time. The algorithm works by defining a recursive function that takes the current search boundaries (low and high indices). At each call, it calculates the middle index and compares the element there to the target. If they match, it returns the index. If the middle element is greater than the target, the function calls itself with the left half (low to mid-1). If smaller, it calls itself with the right half (mid+1 to high). The base case occurs when low exceeds high, indicating the target isn't present, returning -1. The time complexity is O(log n) because each recursive call halves the search space. Space complexity is O(log n) due to the recursive call stack depth. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) by being the complete implementation that uses that recursive function. Common mistakes include: forgetting the base case (causing infinite recursion), using integer overflow when calculating mid (use low + (high-low)//2 instead of (low+high)//2), and not handling empty arrays. The real code pattern is: define recursive function binary_search_rec(arr, target, low, high); if low > high: return -1; mid = low + (high-low)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] > target: return binary_search_rec(arr, target, low, mid-1); else: return binary_search_rec(arr, target, mid+1, high).

examples.trace:
Initial call: binary_search_rec([1,3,5,7,9], 5, 0, 4)
Step 1: low=0, high=4, mid=0+(4-0)//2=2, arr[2]=5 equals target=5 → return 2

Initial call: binary_search_rec([2,4,6,8,10,12], 7, 0, 5)
Step 1: low=0, high=5, mid=0+(5-0)//2=2, arr[2]=6 < 7 → recursive call with low=3, high=5
Step 2: low=3, high=5, mid=3+(5-3)//2=4, arr[4]=10 > 7 → recursive call with low=3, high=3
Step 3: low=3, high=3, mid=3+(3-3)//2=3, arr[3]=8 > 7 → recursive call with low=3, high=2
Step 4: low=3, high=2 → base case low>high → return -1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Search Function
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that searches within given boundaries
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that performs binary search within specified boundaries. The intuition is that recursion naturally models the divide-and-conquer nature of binary search—each call works on a smaller subproblem. The algorithm: function takes array, target, low index, high index. Base case: if low > high, return -1 (not found). Otherwise, calculate mid = low + (high-low)//2. Compare arr[mid] to target. If equal, return mid. If arr[mid] > target, recursively search left half (low to mid-1). If arr[mid] < target, recursively search right half (mid+1 to high). Time complexity per call is O(1), but overall will be O(log n) across all calls. Space complexity is O(log n) for call stack. This feeds into L0 as the main recursive function that gets called. Common mistakes: incorrect base case (should be low > high, not low >= high for consistency), integer overflow in mid calculation (use the safe formula), and not returning the recursive call result. Real code pattern: def rec_search(arr, target, low, high): if low > high: return -1; mid = low + (high-low)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] > target: return rec_search(arr, target, low, mid-1); else: return rec_search(arr, target, mid+1, high).

examples.trace:
Call: rec_search([10,20,30,40,50], 30, 0, 4)
Step 1: low=0, high=4, mid=0+(4-0)//2=2, arr[2]=30 equals target → return 2

Call: rec_search([1,3,5,7,9], 4, 0, 4)
Step 1: low=0, high=4, mid=0+(4-0)//2=2, arr[2]=5 > 4 → recursive call with low=0, high=1
Step 2: low=0, high=1, mid=0+(1-0)//2=0, arr[0]=1 < 4 → recursive call with low=1, high=1
Step 3: low=1, high=1, mid=1+(1-1)//2=1, arr[1]=3 < 4 → recursive call with low=2, high=1
Step 4: low=2, high=1 → base case low>high → return -1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divide Search Interval by Comparing Middle Element
ladders.desc: Halve search space by comparing target to middle element
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique of binary search: at each step, compare the target to the middle element of the current search interval to decide which half to search next. The intuition is that because the array is sorted, comparing to the middle element tells us whether the target could be in the left or right half. If the middle element equals the target, we're done. If it's greater, the target must be in the left half (since all elements to the right are even larger). If it's smaller, the target must be in the right half. This reduces the problem size by half each time. The algorithm: given low and high indices, calculate mid = (low+high)//2 (using safe formula). Compare arr[mid] to target. Based on comparison, either return mid, set high = mid-1 (search left), or set low = mid+1 (search right). Time complexity per step is O(1). This feeds into L1 as the decision logic inside the recursive function. Common mistakes: using incorrect comparison operators, not updating boundaries correctly (must use mid±1 to exclude mid), and off-by-one errors in boundary updates. Real code pattern: mid = low + (high-low)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] > target: high = mid-1; else: low = mid+1.

examples.trace: Step: low=0, high=4, mid=0+(4-0)//2=2, arr[2]=30, target=40 → arr[mid] < target → update low=mid+1=3 (search right half)

Step: low=0, high=4, mid=0+(4-0)//2=2, arr[2]=15, target=10 → arr[mid] > target → update high=mid-1=1
Next step: low=0, high=1, mid=0+(1-0)//2=0, arr[0]=5, target=10 → arr[mid] < target → update low=mid+1=1
Next step: low=1, high=1, mid=1+(1-1)//2=1, arr[1]=10 equals target → found at index 1

ladders.title: Building Block — Calculate Middle Index Safely
ladders.desc: Compute middle index without integer overflow
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves calculating the middle index between two indices safely. The intuition is that we need to find the midpoint to split the search interval, but must avoid integer overflow when indices are large. The algorithm: given low and high indices, compute mid = low + (high - low) // 2. This gives the floor of the average, ensuring it's an integer index. The subtraction (high-low) prevents overflow that could occur with (low+high). Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into L2 as the method to find the middle element for comparison. Common mistakes: using (low+high)//2 which can overflow for large indices, using floating-point arithmetic, or incorrect integer division. Real code pattern: mid = low + (high - low) // 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Element to Target
ladders.desc: Compare array element with target value
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves comparing an element from the array with the target value to determine their relationship. The intuition is that in a sorted array, this comparison tells us where the target might be relative to the current element. The algorithm: given an array element 'x' and target 't', perform one of three comparisons: check if x == t (equal), x > t (greater), or x < t (less). This is typically done with if-elif-else statements. Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into L2 as the comparison that decides which half to search. Common mistakes: using = instead of == for equality, incorrect operator precedence, or not handling all three cases. Real code pattern: if x == t: return 'equal'; elif x > t: return 'greater'; else: return 'less'.

examples.trace: 5 == 5 is true → return 'equal'
8 > 3 is true → return 'greater'

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding how to access elements in an array using indices. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element has a numerical index starting from 0. The algorithm: given an array 'arr' and an index 'i', access the element as arr[i]. This retrieves the value at that position. Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into L4 as how we get the element 'x' to compare. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing (should be 0-based), accessing out-of-bounds indices (causing errors), or confusing index with value. Real code pattern: element = arr[index].

examples.trace: arr[0] is the first element → 10
arr[2] is the third element → 25
